extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box, a heat‑bed as the heating element, a fan to distribute heated air, two temperature sensors inside the box and one sensor outside. The plant is driven by a Raspberry Pi that runs a bang‑bang‑style controller. The goal of the PT is to reach a prescribed temperature inside the box and maintain it despite variations in the content placed inside. Two mathematical models of the PT are defined: Model A (based on four simplifying assumptions) and Model B (identical except that it does not assume all supplied electrical power is converted to air heating).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) provides (1) real‑time visualization of incubator status by streaming sensor and controller data to a time‑series database and rendering it on a dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the PT models by automatically executing the calibration routine described in the case study; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses measured temperatures with Model B; (4) what‑if simulations via co‑simulation that replay historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller parameters; (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioural changes, schedules data‑collection experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations and re‑configures the controller accordingly. These services support monitoring, prediction, optimisation, and control for both developers and operators of the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT implementation relies on a RabbitMQ message broker for asynchronous communication between the Raspberry Pi controller, the low‑level driver, and DT components. The low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator I/O and enforces a safety protocol while publishing temperature readings to RabbitMQ. The controller software implements a bang‑bang control logic with configurable lower/upper temperature limits, heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). Calibration and model execution are performed by dedicated algorithms (including a Kalman filter and co‑simulation engine). All hardware designs, software components and datasets are provided as open‑source artifacts in the public GitHub repository https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The incubator DT follows a centralized message‑bus architecture built around a RabbitMQ server (AMQP). The physical twin (PT) consists of temperature sensors and actuators (heatbed, fan) accessed by a low‑level driver that periodically reads sensor values and publishes them to RabbitMQ. The Raspberry‑Pi controller subscribes to the same broker to receive commands and to publish its control states. Digital‑twin services (real‑time visualisation dashboard, on‑demand calibration, Kalman‑filter based anomaly detection, what‑if co‑simulation) are implemented as separate components that also subscribe to the broker, thereby receiving PT data and issuing configuration or calibration commands. Coordination is achieved by message passing; calibration algorithms update model parameters which are then used by monitors and optimisers. Resource management relies on the RabbitMQ broker to route messages and on a time‑series database for persistence.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is realised through the RabbitMQ message broker using the AMQP protocol, which enables the DT components to listen to controller and driver messages. Sensor data are forwarded to a time‑series database and visualised on a web‑based dashboard. Calibration and anomaly‑detection services are invoked on‑demand via messages, and co‑simulation tools can ingest real‑time or historical data through the same broker, allowing interoperability with external simulation environments and enterprise data stores. The open‑source hardware and software repository on GitHub provides further access for external developers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator Digital Twin incorporates two analytical thermal models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) derived from the heat equation Q = c·m·ΔT and calibrated against measured temperature data. Data are sourced from three temperature sensors (two inside the insulated styrofoam box, one outside) whose readings are collected by a low‑level driver, transmitted via the RabbitMQ message broker, and stored in a time‑series database for real‑time visualization on a dashboard. Calibration algorithms update model parameters using the latest sensor data, while downstream algorithms such as a Kalman filter, anomaly detectors, and optimisation routines consume the calibrated models and data to generate forecasts and control decisions. Model relationships are explicit: sensor data feed calibration; calibrated models feed state estimation and what‑if simulations; all components are orchestrated through the communication server.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a heating element (heatbed) and an air‑circulating fan mounted inside the insulated box. Both actuators are commanded by a Raspberry Pi controller implementing a bang‑bang control strategy with configurable lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C). Commands are dispatched to the low‑level driver via RabbitMQ and the driver enforces a safety protocol before applying the actuation signals. The controller parameters define the actuation range, and the safe‑state mechanism prevents unsafe temperature excursions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of three temperature sensors: two placed inside the box to monitor internal air temperature and one external sensor to record ambient room temperature. Sensor data are periodically read by the low‑level driver, packaged into messages, and published to the RabbitMQ server where they are ingested into the time‑series store for downstream processing. The documents do not specify sampling rates, resolution, or transmission bandwidth, but the architecture enables continuous data flow from the physical twin to the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed through two competing models: Model A assumes all supplied electrical power heats the air, whereas Model B relaxes this assumption to account for heatbed inertia, resulting in higher predictive accuracy as shown in calibration results (Figure 13). Validation is performed by comparing model outputs against measured temperature trajectories and by conducting calibration experiments (Section 3.3). Uncertainty sources include four explicit assumptions: uniform temperature distribution enforced by the fan, negligible wall heat storage, constant specific heat capacity of air, and near‑complete conversion of electrical power to heat (the latter omitted in Model B). Model verification uses the Kalman filter for state estimation and anomaly detection, providing error correction during runtime.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data from three sensors (two internal sensors measuring the incubator air temperature and one external sensor measuring room temperature) are periodically read by the low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi. The driver packages each measurement with a timestamp and sensor identifier into AMQP messages and publishes them to a RabbitMQ message broker. Before transmission the driver applies a safe‑state protocol and, for the DT, may compute the average of the two internal sensors to represent the box temperature. The broker forwards these messages to DT components such as a time‑series database for real‑time visualisation. The exact sampling interval is not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are generated by the DT (or by the DT‑based controller) and sent via RabbitMQ to the low‑level driver. Command messages specify fan actuation (on/off) and heat‑bed parameters (heating duration H, waiting duration C, lower and upper temperature limits LL/UL). The low‑level driver validates each command against its built‑in safe protocol, acknowledges receipt, and executes the actuation on the physical twin. The controller also publishes its current state back to the broker, providing feedback for confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All DT components communicate through a RabbitMQ server using the AMQP protocol over TCP/IP. The architecture follows a publish‑subscribe pattern where the low‑level driver and controller publish sensor and state topics, and DT services subscribe to them. RabbitMQ is described as a mature, lightweight, and easy‑to‑deploy message broker that can run on‑premise or in the cloud, offering reliable message delivery; specific bandwidth, latency, or security protocol details are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The controller and low‑level driver are hosted on a Raspberry Pi attached to the incubator. RabbitMQ, the central communication backbone, can be deployed either on‑premise or in the cloud as mentioned in the documents. DT services such as visualisation dashboards, calibration modules, and anomaly detection run as separate software components that connect to the broker. The documents do not specify exact computational or storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or detailed availability/reliability assurances beyond the inherent reliability of RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The incubation digital twin is constructed by integrating calibration algorithms that update the cyber‑physical models with the latest parameter estimates (Document 1). The development pipeline includes: (1) real‑time data acquisition from the Physical Twin (temperature sensors) via a low‑level driver that publishes readings to a RabbitMQ message broker (Document 5); (2) storage of these time‑stamped measurements in a time‑series database and rendering on a dashboard for visualisation (Document 4); (3) on‑demand model calibration performed automatically by invoking the calibration routine described in Section 3.3 of the case study (Document 4); (4) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses Model B with live sensor data (Document 4); (5) what‑if simulation executed as co‑simulation that consumes historical or live data from the PT to evaluate alternative controller settings (Document 4); and (6) a self‑adaptation loop that, upon detection of a behavioural change, schedules experiments, re‑configures the controller, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations and finally applies the new controller parameters (Document 4). Version control and evolution management are implicit in the automated calibration and the loop that continuously re‑trains the models as new data become available.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin provides decision support through several insight mechanisms: (i) anomaly detection identifies deviations between measured temperatures and model‑predicted temperatures; (ii) a Kalman filter supplies an estimate of the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature, enabling heat‑bed state estimation; (iii) what‑if simulations allow the evaluation of alternative controller parameter sets to optimise heating performance; (iv) the self‑adaptation loop uses the detected anomalies to trigger a sequence of actions—experiment scheduling, data acquisition, model re‑calibration, Kalman filter re‑configuration, simulation‑based optimisation, and controller re‑configuration—thereby autonomously adjusting the system. Users interact with the twin via a real‑time dashboard that visualises sensor data, model predictions, and detected anomalies, supporting human‑in‑the‑loop decisions when needed.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Incubator DT incorporates safety measures through a 'safe protocol' embedded in the low‑level driver, which periodically reads temperature data, validates incoming commands from the controller, and ensures the physical twin remains in a safe state before forwarding commands. Communication between the controller, low‑level driver, and other DT components is performed via a RabbitMQ message broker, providing reliable message delivery, though the documents do not specify authentication, encryption, or access‑control mechanisms for the broker.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
